TextENGNew: The shopping district was packed in the early evening—packed enough to make you wonder if every housewife in Hinamizawa had gathered there.
changeReason: Old says 'shopping center'; new says 'shopping district'; Japanese 商店街 is a shopping district/street.

TextENGNew: Dinner preparation time being what it is, that impression might not have been all that far from the truth.

A grandmother with a curved back adding vegetables to her basket. Housewives chatting at the register while stuffing groceries into plastic bags. A young mother strolling through the produce aisle hand-in-hand with her child.
changeReason: Old says 'young couple'; new says 'young mother'; Japanese 若奥様 means young wife, not couple. Also changes 'children's hands' (plural) to 'child' (singular).

TextENGNew: Housewives of all shapes and ages filled the aisles.
I walked beside Rena with a plastic basket in hand, throwing in one ingredient after another as she directed.
changeReason: Old says 'plastic bag'; new says 'plastic basket'; Japanese かご means basket.

TextENGNew: "Hmm... what do you think you and your dad would like? Like?"
changeReason: Old renders Rena's verbal tic by duplicating 'Keiichi-kun'; new renders it as 'Like?', changing representation of the speech pattern.

TextENGNew: Rena glanced at me as we headed toward the meat section.
changeReason: Old says 'sidelong glance'; new says 'glanced at me'; Japanese のぞき込みながら means peering at face, not sidelong.

TextENGNew: "Anything is fine. Especially as far as Dad is concerned."
"Huh?"
"That goofball would eat whatever you made and tell you it was the best thing he'd ever had."
changeReason: Old says 'That clown'; new says 'That goofball'; different characterization for スチャラカ親父.

TextENGNew: "Hau~, but..."
"Don't worry. I know firsthand how good your cooking is. Whatever you make, you'll hear no complaints from me."
"Ah, thank you... hau☆"
Rena's face went scarlet. I'd meant every word—there wasn't an ounce of teasing in it—but maybe that sincerity was precisely what made her blush even more.
"Then I'll make curry♪"
Rena began selecting from the neatly arranged cuts of beef, choosing what looked most delicious for the best value.
I stood alongside her, looking over the appealingly sliced and cut pieces of meat. The prices and portions varied, but they all looked fairly appetizing.

Rena stood beside me with one finger on her chin, her eyes moving back and forth as she earnestly searched for the best cut. I thought to myself—it must be that same care and dedication in choosing ingredients that makes her cooking so good. A ridiculously simple equation, but it crossed my mind anyway.
Someday Rena will marry someone, become a mother, and put her skill to work for her family.

Her husband and children will savor every dish she makes. The dining table will overflow with smiles and happiness. ...For some reason, I found myself with an overpowering desire to hurt—no, to envy—whoever that husband would turn out to be.
"Oh, this one~♪"
Rena's happy exclamation pulled me out of my ridiculous daydream. She'd picked up a box of curry roux and was checking the price.
I looked at the packaging. The photo was purely aspirational, but Rena would surely make something even better than what was pictured.
"All right, let's go with this then—here, Keiichi-kun—...?"
Rena extended her right hand to place the box in the basket, so I tilted it toward her. ...But Rena's hand froze in midair, perfectly still.
Puzzled, I looked at her face. She was looking past me, back the way we'd come.
No—she wasn't just 'looking.' She was staring.
"What's wrong, Rena?"
Almost as soon as I spoke, a sharp, aggressive smell hit my nose. This was... perfume?

I turned around with a start. A woman in a conspicuously flashy outfit stood there—extravagant clothes, extravagant makeup—clearly not one of the neighborhood housewives. She looked at Rena and spoke with a bright smile.
"Oh, it is you after all—Reina-chan."
"Ah, Rina-san. Good evening~."

Rena returned the greeting pleasantly. Did they know each other? Not following any of this, I found myself sandwiched between them, turning my eyes back and forth between Rena and this woman called Rina.
"Oh, why are you out shopping? Didn't we say the three of us were going to dinner tonight?"
"I'm sorry, something came up..."
"Oh, really? We went to all the trouble of getting a good table, too."
"I'm sorry... Ah, but it would be a waste to cancel entirely. Please go with Dad, just the two of you."
"I suppose that's what we'll do, then. Heehee."
A smile spread across Rina-san's face.
From what I gathered listening in, Rena had apparently made plans to go to dinner somewhere with this Rina-san and with Rena's father—the three of them together.
...Then why had she suddenly offered to cook dinner at our house? Shouldn't she be prioritizing the plans she already had?
"Still, I'm curious. What was this thing that suddenly came up?"
Rina-san asked, her smile not quite reaching the register of 'refined.'
"It's a penalty for our club. Right, Keiichi-kun?♪"
Rena turned to me for confirmation, her voice a deliberate cheerfulness.
"Huh? Oh... yeah..."
I gave a vague answer to the sudden question.
Rina-san gave me a briefly blank look at that, then smiled and fixed me with a long, sizing-up sort of stare.
"I see, I see~. So dinner with your future husband is more important, is it~?"
"F-future husband... hau~☆"
Rena went pink, then scarlet.
...But.

Something about it was different from the way she normally went red when I teased her. I couldn't put it precisely into words, but there was something off about this particular 'hau~.' A vague, faint wrongness settled somewhere in my chest as I looked at her face.
"Well, I'll be off, then. Let's all have dinner together sometime—the three of us. I have all sorts of things I want to talk to you about, Reina-chan."
"Yes. I'll look forward to it."
"Oh—if you're making curry, this one is delicious. Highly recommend. Well, see you."
Rina-san said, deftly plucking one of the curry roux boxes from the shelf and dropping it into our basket, then walked past us with a smile and a wave.
"...Rena?"
Rena's eyes had been following Rina-san all the way until she turned the corner and disappeared from view.
"I don't really like this one."
"This is Rena's special recommended curry☆ Hau~♪"
As if nothing had happened, Rena took the box Rina-san had put in our basket and returned it to the shelf, then placed the one she'd already been holding into the basket instead.
"We're getting meat too, right, Keiichi-kun?"
"Hm? ...Oh, yeah."
And just like that.
Rena's face had returned to that same warm, friendly smile as always.
The bright kitchen light fell over the dinner table.
Beef curry, soup, and salad had been set out on the table in portions for three.
Each of us reached our chopsticks toward the spread in front of us, one bite after another. As expected of Rena—both the presentation and the taste were exceptional.
"So not only is Rena-chan adorable, she can cook too! Isn't that remarkable! Ha-ha-ha-ha-ha!"

Dad had been in exceptionally high spirits for a while now. Having a girl at our dinner table seemed to delight him enormously. Rena sat there eating the beef curry she'd made, cheeks flushed with shy embarrassment.
"Oh, if Rena-chan were to become my son's bride, I'd be the happiest man alive~"
"Pbfft! Ge—cough—!!"
The bombshell Dad dropped made me nearly spit out the soup I was drinking.
"Wh-what are you saying, Dad!! Why would Rena and I—!!"
"Hm? But you two get along so well, don't you? I'd more or less assumed you were already like that..."
"S-something like that... hauuu☆"
"No, Rena!! This is not the time to be turning red!! If you go all shy, you're playing right into Dad's hands!!"
I grabbed Rena's shoulders and shook her, but white steam was already puffing from the top of her head, her ears as red as cherries, and all she could murmur was a dazed 'bride... bride...'
"I'm volunteering if my son doesn't measure up."
"Would you please be quiet, you clown!!"
"Wha-hah-hah! 'Clown,' he calls his own father! That's something, Keiichi!"
Being praised by a father who was aggressively pursuing my friend made me about as happy as a rainstorm.

Having my friend witness this kind of embarrassing family comedy—this was the greatest blunder of Maebara Keiichi's life. I looked over to Rena trying to think of some kind of excuse, but she was still out of action, smoke rising from her head.
She was still muttering incoherently, but at least she'd missed the more incriminating lines—small mercy.
"Bride... bride... Dad's... bride..."
"Nooo!! Please pretend you didn't hear that!!"

This was Ryūgū Rena, and she was not to be underestimated... I spent the next thirty minutes after she came back to her senses explaining to her in painstaking detail that I was the only normal person in this household.
And with that, our lively dinner drew to a close. Afterward, the three of us sat together talking about one thing and another over the tea Rena had brewed for us.
Our family made up most of the subject matter, but Rena participated actively, weaving in the softness particular to girls and letting the conversation breathe in ways it couldn't before.
Rena was good at both talking and listening—which was probably why she could hold a natural conversation even with my parents, who were so much older than her. In truth, that was a remarkable thing.
I doubt I could do the same, if pressed.
It was striking how much more variety opened up in a conversation that usually flowed between just the three of us, simply because one girl had joined in.
I felt a small, quiet sense of wonder at that—the small change Rena brought, and how enormous the reaction it sparked seemed to be.
"What sort of pictures does Keiichi-kun's dad paint?"
Rena brought up the topic that had come up on our walk home from school.
"Hm? Are you interested in art, Rena-chan?"
Dad put down his coffee cup and turned an unnecessarily dashing smile on Rena. ...Actually, had I ever seen him smile like that before?
"Oh, not that much exactly. I was just wondering what kind of things you paint..."
"Well, maybe I'll show you sometime. When Keiichi isn't around. Wha-ha-ha!"
"Why does it have to be when I'm not there??"
I shot back immediately.
"Well, naturally... they're not the sort of paintings to show to a boy your age!"
Dad pounded the table with a closed fist and made his case with fire blazing in his eyes, as though my asking were some grave offense.
"Even if that were the actual reason, you can't just say it out loud like that..."
"Ahahahaha~"
Rena laughed—a bright, rolling laugh.

A natural smile, different from the one she'd shown when meeting Rina-san at the shop earlier. ...Right. That was Rena's real smile. I felt something loosen inside me—like a fish bone that had been lodged in my chest finally working free.
"Well, I'll be heading home now~"
Putting on her shoes at the entryway, Rena called back into the living room toward my father.
"Right. ...Thanks for everything today."

I said it with a playfully awkward salute. Dad had apparently gone to his studio to show Rena something, but knowing him, it couldn't possibly be anything decent—so I'd chosen this moment to see her off.
Besides, it was past eight o'clock. If she didn't leave soon, she'd have a hard time getting ready for tomorrow.
Come to think of it, Rena's father had gone out to eat tonight... with that Rina woman. But it was already this late—surely he was home by now.
Even after our encounter with Rina-san at the shop, Rena never said a single word about her.
But I didn't sense any strong insistence on not talking about her—more that I genuinely couldn't tell what Rena actually thought of her at all.
Normally, just listening to two people talk, you could sense naturally from the atmosphere whether they got along, or whether they were putting on a polite face while secretly disliking each other.
But when Rena and Rina-san were talking, nothing came through at all.
If I were to describe it: it was as if they were speaking through a thin film of cellophane.
Maybe Rina-san was someone who kept other people at arm's length by nature, and Rena, knowing that, was simply maintaining a careful, measured distance.
But... there was that one thing Rena had let slip while we were washing up after dinner.
"...Was it really all right? It was a family dinner—"
"—It wasn't family. She's just a friend of Dad's."
What did she mean by those words?
Wanting to find some opening that would let me bring it up with her, I called out to Rena as she stood at the entryway putting on her shoes.
"Do you want me to walk you home?"
The distance wasn't long, but it was dark out. I thought if I walked with her, maybe we'd have a chance to talk.
"No, that's okay."
Rena answered with a smile as she pulled on her shoes.
"I'll just run home—I'll be fine."
Gentle as her smile was, the words themselves carried a clear refusal.
She tapped the toe of her shoe against the floor a few times, then turned her back to me.
'I want to go home alone...'—I felt her slender back silently saying as much, and the words dried up in my throat.
"See you."
"Ah, yeah."
Usually I'd have something more coherent to say, but all that came out was something limp and hollow.
I wanted at least a promise—that tomorrow we'd share the same kind of laughter and warmth as always. And so I put far more feeling into that routine parting line than I ever normally would.
"See you tomorrow, okay?"
"Yeah, see you tomorrow."

Rena answered with her back still turned, her hand reaching for the doorknob. Her hand paused for just a moment—and before I could even register the '?' forming in my mind, Rena turned around and spoke.
"Thanks for today."
A warm, billowing smile—the kind that simply warmed your heart to look at. The wordless, heavy sadness that had wrapped around me dissolved in an instant.
If human expressions had a price attached, even the national budget couldn't pay for this girl's smile.
I'd been trying to think of how to encourage her, and instead she'd encouraged me. I smiled wryly at my own shallowness and at the strength in Rena that exceeded all my expectations.
Rena opened the front door and stepped out. I put on my own shoes and followed her out to see her off. With a small 'see you,' she gave a little wave and set off down the path toward her house.
'Thanks for today'... I'm the one who should be thanking you.
Swallowing a wry smile, I waved back—until her figure disappeared into the darkness and the motion of her hand was gone.
"...Don't push yourself too hard."
Rena had been hooking her heel into her shoe—her slender white fingers paused for just a fraction of a second.
It could only have been about one second that she'd been still.
But it felt to me like an impossibly long time—a stretch of aching suspense.
As if to shake off that one still second, Rena tapped the toe of her shoe against the floor a few more times and pulled it on. Then she slowly raised her face and looked at me.
'How did you know? ...How did you?'
Rena was silent, but the questioning, bewildered expression that asked that spoke to me plainly.
Don't be ridiculous. No matter how well you hide it, I can tell.
You didn't want to have dinner with Rina-san.
That was why, at that moment, you held out a smile that wasn't smiling, and made me lie for you.
It's not something I'm particularly proud of, but when it comes to making you laugh, or flustering you, or watching you squirm—I have a fair amount of confidence in myself.
My jokes. Mion's quips. Satoko's guilelessness. Rika-chan's sweetness. I've seen your real, whole-hearted laughter so many times.
That's why I can tell instantly—whether the smile on your face is the real one from somewhere deep inside you, or just a surface-level lie.
I said all of this in silence, the same way Rena had done earlier.
Taking in those unspoken words, Rena dropped her gaze. Her fingers curled tight around the hem of her pale skirt—the folds gathering between them as though mirroring the turmoil inside her heart.
I patted Rena gently on the shoulder and said,
"—Let me walk you home."
Rena gave a small nod, face still turned down.
Outside, the darkness was striking—deeper than you'd expect.

The air in Hinamizawa was cleaner than anywhere I'd lived before, so the stars were countless and vivid in the sky. And yet tonight the dark felt so vast and absolute that even all that starlight seemed swallowed by it.
We walked side by side along the road to Rena's house, neither of us saying a word.
If we'd been chattering about nothing—the way we did on the way home from school—maybe the sky would have felt a little lighter.
But because neither of us said anything, the black of the sky felt truly, completely black—and that darkness seeped into my chest, slowly and steadily.
From the way Rena had acted at my doorway just now, it wasn't hard to guess that something was weighing on her.
But without knowing what kind of trouble it was—or what situation had given rise to it—I couldn't find a single word to offer her.
"Tell me what happened."
It would be no use saying that. Rena was far more stubborn than she appeared, and once she'd decided she didn't want to say something, no amount of prompting from me would get it out of her.
Nor would 'hang in there' accomplish anything—hollow words from someone who didn't know the situation weren't comfort, just noise.
Most of all, Rena had said nothing and kept silent. That silence itself was a declaration—she didn't want me pressing any further into this.
I wondered whether she planned to carry this on her own—without telling Mion, Satoko, or Rika-chan. Without telling anyone at all.
The crunch of gravel underfoot echoed around us.
Rena was walking right beside me—and yet she felt impossibly far away. Unable to find any words to close that distance, I was seized by a helpless, gnawing frustration.
In the end, we walked the whole way in silence, all the way to Rena's house.
We stopped in front of the entryway. I thought I heard a sigh from beside me, and I turned toward Rena.
She stood perfectly still, facing the unlit entranceway with a face empty of expression.
No light was coming from inside the house either. Rena's father hadn't returned yet from his dinner with Rina. Standing there in the dark, Rena's house looked as if it were asleep.
Had that sleeping house been holding its breath, waiting for someone to come home? Waiting in silence and darkness for someone to open the door?
Staring at the empty, unlit house, I felt a thread of anger toward Rena's father—a man I'd never even met.

Why are you out eating dinner with a woman Rena clearly doesn't want to be around, while your daughter is sitting here alone with all of this weighing on her? Why can't you be a father about it—sit with her, hear her out, and then...
"Thank you for walking me home."
Rena's voice cut straight through my thoughts.
The timing was so perfect it gave me a small start.
I was almost tempted to read into it—to hear a quiet reproach in those words: don't say anything worse about Dad.
But Rena's face held a soft, floating smile.
It was a gentle and quietly heartbreaking smile—something like a faint light glowing in the deep dark around us.
"Well, see you."
She dropped that spare goodbye and started walking toward the door.
Something in her retreating back gave off just the faintest feeling—a wordless wish for me to go home soon.
She didn't want me to keep staring at this sleeping house. And she probably didn't want me reading too much into everything I'd felt tonight.

It's true that everyone carries things they can't talk about. I knew far less about Rena than I thought, and I had no idea how heavy her burden was, what kind of burden it was, or how to help her with it.
But beyond all that reasoning—I just didn't want her bearing this alone. Because her back right now looked so lonely. And I didn't want to see that loneliness in her back ever again.
I had no intention of prying more than was needed. If I were to forcibly tear open the scab on her heart that no one was meant to touch, she would only suffer from the pain of it.
But if Rena ever felt she wanted to talk to someone... I wanted to be there beside her in that moment. Because we were—
"...We're friends, you know."
I'd meant to hold it back, but only those last words made it out.
My words broke the silence. Rena stopped and turned around.
"I'm always here for you, for anything. So—don't push yourself too hard, okay?"
I tried to say it with the most gentle, dependable face I could manage.
Rena held my gaze steadily—as if reading something from my expression.
I held hers in return. We might never have looked at each other quite this seriously before. A subtle, unfamiliar tension was beginning to settle over the moment—when,
Rena softly broke into a smile. The air around us eased all at once.
"Goodbye, Keiichi-kun."

She gave a small flutter of her right hand and turned on her heel, smiling still. The same gesture, the same smile as always—and yet something was unmistakably tucked away inside that farewell beyond a simple goodbye.
Had she smiled in answer to 'we're friends'? ...Or was it a forced smile, thrown up to shake something off?
Which was it—what had been hiding behind Rena's smile? That was the question I kept turning over in my mind as I watched her figure disappear beyond her front door.
